RIP Maestro. In this explosive 1984 live performance from Toronto, Kazuhito Yamashita (1961-2026) performs his own transcription of Mussorgskyâs Pictures at an Exhibition. He creates a sonic landscape so vast and thunderous that he truly sounds like a full orchestra contained within six strings. 

He shreds the classical guitar rule book, flinging his body across the instrument with a flying left elbow and overextended hands. This level of volume and orchestral color is not human! He even tunes his strings several times without missing a single beat of the musical narrative.

Kazuhito Yamashita, born in Nagasaki in 1961, rose to fame by refusing to be limited by âexisting guitar technique.â His 1981 recording of this Mussorgsky masterpiece was so revolutionary that it was awarded the prestigious Deutsche Grammophon Award. His technical facility is nothing short of phenomenal, particularly a right-hand technique that allows for a staggering range of dynamics, from the most delicate whispers to the bone-rattling bells of âThe Great Gate of Kiev.â Beyond this tour de force, Yamashitaâs discography is massive, including the complete works of Fernando Sor on 16 CDs and his own transcriptions of Bachâs solo violin and cello works.

Mussorgskyâs original piano suite was meant to depict a walk through an art gallery, but Yamashitaâs version adds a visceral, almost tactile dimension to the paintings. When he hunches his shoulders and digs into the wood, he is channeling the raw, rugged Russian spirit that Mussorgsky intended before Ravel smoothed it over with his famous orchestral version. I hope Yamashitaâs legacy stays alive because he embodied the fact that in order to capture the spirit of the music, one has to be entirely free from the constraints of the physical.

Stand-up comedy is already one of the hardest art forms because itâs raw, live, and unforgiving. Add dark humor into the mix, and the risk multiplies. Dark jokes walk on a thin line between honesty and offense, truth and discomfort, laughter and silence. One wrong word, wrong timing, or wrong audience, and the room can turn cold. Thatâs what makes it powerful. Dark comedy isnât meant to be safe. Itâs meant to expose what people are afraid to talk about.

For a comic, carrying both stand-up and dark humor together requires intelligence, control, and emotional awareness. Youâre not just telling jokes. Youâre managing tension. Youâre reading the room. Youâre choosing when to push and when to pull back. Dark humor works only when the audience feels that the comedian understands the weight of the subject. Without that awareness, it feels careless. With it, it feels fearless.

What makes dark comedy so effective is that it turns pain into relief. It gives people permission to laugh at things theyâre usually told to hide, suppress, or fear. Laughter becomes a release valve. A way to process trauma, anxiety, and absurdity without pretending everything is fine. It doesnât glorify darkness. It disarms it.

Thatâs why it works with the masses. Not because people are cruel, but because people are tired of pretending. Dark humor says what everyone thinks but no one wants to admit. It breaks social tension. It humanizes uncomfortable truths.

The best comedians donât use darkness to shock.
They use it to connect.

Sludge. Itâs not exactly super sexy, but what you do with it is important.

Brewing creates three main organic waste streams: malt, hops and yeast.
Our spent malt already gets repurposed as animal feed (and occasional crackers and dog biscuits), but sludge (the thick slurry of leftover hop material and yeast) has historically been a trickier problem to solve.
For a long time, we collected it in tanks, but it still ended up being trucked to landfill as liquid waste. Not great.

Enter: The Sludge Tank.

In 2024, funding from the Wellington City Council Waste Minimisation Seed Fund - Organics Diversion Fund allowed us to build a proper system, designed by our own Steve Almond (Sustainability & Projects Manager) in collaboration with EnviroNZ. Sludge is now pumped from our fermenters into a holding tank, collected by EnviroNZ, and composted by Capital Compost, turning it into potting mix and soil for public use.

The impact?

 ð± 60+ tonnes of organic waste diverted from landfill per year
 ð± 5+ tonnes of CO2 emissions prevented

Very cool, very satisfying. End of story, right?....Wrong!

 After 8 months of doing this, we noticed somethingâ¦ the sludge smells incredible! Concentrated hop aroma, bright, fruity, punchy. Too good to compost, and you know that here at Garage Project, we're always wondering if things can be made into beer, and this sludge was no different. We gave hope a chance, and we are so thrilled to announce that our recycling experiment was a delicious success!

Introducing:

Sludgefest, Radical Recycled Hop IPA - 9.5%

This tasty beer is brewed by giving those leftover dry hops a second life before they make their noble exit to compost. Multiple doses of leftover dry hop âsludgeâ, still loaded with hop aroma and flavour, pumped straight from other beers into this tank of Sludgefest making it technically our most heavily hopped beer ever. Wasteâs only waste if you waste it, and, trust us, You wonât want to waste a drop.

In 1930, long before âviralâ meant anything but a flu, Wilbur Hall was already breaking the internet of his day â the cinema â with his unforgettable act in King of Jazz. 

In that legendary excerpt, Hall plays âPop Goes the Weaselâ in a rather astonishing acrobatic way. But he wasnât just a clownish entertainer: he was a phenomenal musician. A gifted trombonist, violinist, and showman, he had been performing musical gags since the 1920s and soon became a key figure in Paul Whitemanâs band, one of the most famous jazz orchestras of the time. 

His blend of humor, technical precision, and musicality made him a true pioneer of musical comedy â a bridge between jazz virtuosity and vaudeville genius.

Later, Hall joined Spike Jones and His City Slickers, a group famous for turning classical and popular tunes into hilarious, perfectly timed sound spectacles. Jones only hired the best of the best, and Hallâs mastery and comic timing were unmatched. Every gag, every breath, every note was timed to the second.

Have you ever seen a video where the beat of a song lines up perfectly with someone walking, a ball bouncing, or even a door slamming? That oddly satisfying feeling isnât just in your imaginationâyour brain is wired to love it. Psychologists call this perceptual synchrony, which means we naturally get a little dopamine hit when sound and visuals align in perfect timing. Itâs the same reason why clapping in rhythm at a concert feels good or why dancing to music feels so natural. When an edit âperfectly matches the song,â your brain treats it like solving a mini puzzleâit sees order in chaos, and that sense of harmony feels rewarding.

On social media, this effect is everywhere. Editors deliberately cut clips to match beats because they know it hooks attention. Even if you didnât plan to watch the whole video, your brain gets tricked into staying because itâs subconsciously waiting for the next perfect sync. Itâs like ASMR for your eyes and ears working together. Whatâs wild is that people often comment âthis goes too wellâ because it feels almost magical, like the universe itself had rhythm. In reality, itâs just your brain celebrating perfect timing.

Recipe

1 Thorâs hammer beef shin joint (I used a 4kg joint)
20g Mediterranean rub
250g beef stock

1. cut away any excess fat and sinew from the beef, tying up the joint with butcherâs twine so it holds its shape.
2. Render down the fat you just cut off and brush that tallow on to the joint. Follow that up with a good covering of the rub youâre using. I do all this in a baking tray which Iâll also smoke the joint in.
3. Smoke at 225Â°F (107Â°C) until it hits 170Â°F (76Â°C) internal. This will take around 4 to 5 hours.
4. Pour the beef stock into the baking tray and cover the whole thing with foil. Continue to cook in the smoker/grill or transfer to the oven at the same temperature until it hits 205Â°F (96Â°C).
5. Open up the foil to allow the steam to escape then loosely close and allow to rest for 1 hour.

4. Go in with the red wine and reduce down by half and add in the meat, crushed tomatoes, passata, beef stock, and a boquet garni of all the fresh herbs.
5. Cook until itâs reduced to a thickness you want for the lasagna. Personally I like it to be very thick to avoid a watery lasagne.

Recipe â¤µï¸â¤µï¸â¤µï¸

Before we dive into this recipe, I have to give a shoutout to @bearmountainbbq âIâve been using their pellets for the past 5 years, and there is a reason I do! Their smoke pellets add incredible depth and flavor to everything I cook. These Pellets can be used in ANY Smoker! For this cook I used Maple Bourbon Pecan 

Makes 12 
Ingredients:
1 lb 80/20 Ground Beef
6 Yellow Onions
2 Tbsp Butter
1 Tbsp Worcestershire
2 Tbsp SPG Seasoning
1/2 Cup Chopped Pickles
8 Ounces Cheddar Cheese
24 Bacon Slices
4 Tbsp BBQ Rub - Sticky Bourbon Brown Ale from @overthefirecooking 
6 Tbsp BBQ Sauce
Sesame Seeds

In & Out Sauce:
1/2 Cup Mayo
3 Tbsp Ketchup
2 Tbsp @melindasfoods Sriracha
2 Tbsp Relish
1 Tbsp Apple Cider Vinegar or White Vinegar
1 Tsp Sugar
2 Tbsp Water to thin

How to:
1. Using the Onion Rings you did not use. Chop about 1 cup and sautÃ© in a pan with 2 Tbsp Butter and 1 Tbsp Worcestershire for about 8 minutes until soft 
2. In a bowl add Ground Beef, SautÃ©ed Onions, SPG Seasoning, Pickles, Cheddar Cheese, and mix well
3. Cut ends of Onions and slice in the center and use a big and smaller ring to stuff
4. Stuff in the mixture and use 2 slices of bacon to cover the rings
5. Season with your favorite BBQ Rub
6. In the Smoker at 250 degrees for about 1 1/2 hours
7. Then baste with BBQ and top with sesame seeds and back in the smoker for about 15 minutes
8. Dip in the In & Out Sauce and enjoy!

Here are the top 10 Sydney food spots you must visit right now! Harris Park, Sydney's Little India. This place is a paradise for anyone who loves Indian food. Next is Cabramatta. This spot is famous for Sydney Vietnamese street food including authentic pho and crispy bahn mi. Auburn is a hub for international flavours. Think kebabs, halal snack packs and some of the most delicious baklava you'll ever taste! Burwood is the go-to spot for authentic Chinese cuisien. From dumplings to beef noodles, this area is packed with delicious options. For incredible Afghani, Persian and Lebanese food Merrylands is a Sydney foodie hidden gem. Lakemba is famous for its Ramadan night market but you can find delicious halal Middle Eastern and Asian food here all year round. Campsie is the place to go for Malaysian food lovers. Think steaming bowls of laksa, stir fried noodles and the sweet and refreshing drinks like sirap bandung. Rooty Hill and Doonside offer a taste of the Philippines with dishes like Lechon, pancit and halo halo. Fairfield is another Sydney foodie hidden gem full of international flavours from Peruvian empanadas to Laotian ox tongue delicacies. Granville, known for its iconic Lebanese food. The mixed plates here are second to none. Your taste buds will thank you after visiting these Sydney food hotspots!

Also known as beer poking or spiking, this 400+ year tradition has been making a comeback lately. This involves heating a hot poker and dipping it into your beer to create an instant caramelization altering the flavor to create a creamy, toasty treat that is perfect for cold weather. (Check out @joshmbernstein recent article in @imbibe for more info.)

Some people use a butane torch, or, a pizza oven to heat their poker red hot. You can also create a similar effect by making a steinbier with a hot rock.

During the Soviet era, the government heavily restricted access to Western media, but citizens developed creative ways to access forbidden content. One of the most notable workarounds was the emergence of "bone music" or "ribs" - bootleg records made from discarded X-ray plates that contained banned Western music. People also engaged in "samizdat" - the clandestine copying and distribution of banned literature and news. Additionally, despite jammers blocking Western radio broadcasts like Radio Free Europe and Voice of America, many Soviets learned to tune in during certain hours or locations where signals were stronger. This created an underground culture of information sharing that helped maintain connections to Western culture despite official censorship.

What a week, maybe we have something for you... Although it's dark and freezing cold in Finland for almost half the year, the people there are among the happiest in the world. First place in the World Happiness Report seven times in a row - what is their secret? 

Perhaps it's the high quality of life, the endless forests or the many saunas. But certainly also because of KalsarikÃ¤nnit. Sounds complicated, but means something like: âGetting drunk at home in your knickersâ. 

A word that perfectly summarises how you can experience maximum enjoyment with minimum effort - alone, relaxed, no frills. No wonder this philosophy has so much to do with Finland's relaxed attitude to life. Instead of the eternal pursuit of perfection, Finns indulge in pleasure, irrationality and a little intoxication. 

Perhaps this is precisely the antithesis of our optimisation mania: simply letting go and celebrating life. Is KalsarikÃ¤nnit perhaps something for you?

If you love a light, umami ramen you have to try this little ramen eatery in Pyrmont ð

They use chicken based broth and shoyu only, no MSG. And only 3 kinds are available on the menu- bonito, dried sardine and a spicy niboshi. 

They have a unique broth making technique using the coffee siphons to create a delicate, complex soup. 

I ordered their signature- The bonito ramen. It's made over the syphon individually. It is presented simply, the taste is light and aromatic, a touch smokey too with a dried bonito, mackerel and kombu base. 

The spicy niboshi (dried sardine) ramen is a favorite of mine. It is light, flavorful from the sardine broth and kombu with some of their original chili oil. The sous vide chicken chashu was a great accompaniment. 

Orders 
Katsuo (bonito) ramen $24
Spicy niboshi (dried sardine) ramen $24 
Chicken karaage $14

Potato Parcel is a quirky and unique service that allows people to send custom messages or images printed on potatoes, making for a humorous and memorable gift.

Founded in 2015, it gained widespread attention after appearing on Shark Tank, where entrepreneur Mark Cuban invested in the business. 

The concept is simple yet entertaining: users choose a potato, add their personalized message or photo, and the company delivers it to the recipient.

Potato Parcel has become a viral sensation, proving that even the most unconventional ideas can find success in niche markets.
